data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "A pan-Mediterranean pandemic or novel pathogen selectively decimated palace-dependent populations while Egyptian medical infrastructure and geographic isolation provided immunity buffers", "mechanism": "Disease transmission networks followed trade routes connecting palace centers; centralized grain storage and dense administrative populations created superspreader conditions. Egyptian delta geography and quarantine practices (documented in medical papyri) limited penetration, while decentralized Nubian/Kushite supply chains provided fallback.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Egyptian survival"], "excludes": ["Sea Peoples as primary cause", "climate as primary cause", "Greek Dark Age social transformation", "Neo-Hittite successor states"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Pandemic burial assemblages or epidemic mortality profiles in palace destruction layers", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian medical papyri or tomb inscriptions documenting unusual mortality events c. 1200-1150 BCE", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Genomic or aDNA evidence of pathogen presence in Eastern Mediterranean skeletal remains from this period", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Ugaritic or Hittite texts describing plague or illness preceding destruction", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Deliberately atypical: no major historian has proposed disease as primary Late Bronze Age collapse mechanism. Attempts to import Antonine Plague/Black Death analogies anachronistically. High risk of falsification via absence-of-evidence."}
